name: Mordant Fixation Feedback Mechanics
executive_summary:
Mordant Fixation Feedback Mechanics (MFFM) transfers the chemistry of mordant dyeing -- where metallic salt mordants form coordinate-covalent bonds between dye molecules and textile fibers, fundamentally altering the resulting color based on which mordant is used -- to the domain of board game design. In textile dyeing, the same natural dye (e.g., cochineal) produces dramatically different colors depending on the mordant: alum mordant yields bright scarlet, iron mordant yields deep purple-black, tin mordant yields vivid orange, and copper mordant yields warm brown. The mordant is a minority component by mass but the dominant determinant of the final color experience. The dye provides the raw chromophore; the mordant determines how that chromophore is fixed, oriented, and presented to the viewer.

MFFM proposes that core game mechanics function as mordants for thematic content: the same theme (e.g., Mediterranean trade) produces radically different player experiences depending on the core mechanic through which it is expressed. Mediterranean trade expressed through auction mechanics produces competitive tension, risk assessment, and bluffing dynamics; expressed through route-building mechanics it produces spatial planning, network optimization, and territorial competition; expressed through worker-placement it produces resource allocation, timing pressure, and opportunity cost awareness. The theme is the dye; the mechanic is the mordant; the player experience is the resulting color. MFFM argues that game designers should select the core mechanic first (choose the mordant), then apply the theme (the dye), rather than the common practice of developing theme first and selecting mechanics to fit -- because the mechanic, not the theme, is the primary determinant of player experience.

The framework generates a testable prediction: in blind playtests where players experience games with identical themes but different core mechanics, player experience ratings (engagement, tension, satisfaction, strategic depth) will cluster by mechanic type rather than by theme type. Players will describe two auction games with different themes as 'feeling similar' more readily than they describe two games with the same theme but different mechanics as 'feeling similar.' This mechanic-dominance effect is analogous to mordant dominance in dyeing: the mordant determines the color family more than the dye does, just as the mechanic determines the experience family more than the theme does.

mapped_components.unit: A single player decision within a game turn: what to bid, where to place a worker, which route to claim, what to trade. Each decision is shaped by the core mechanic (mordant) into a specific cognitive-emotional experience. The same thematic decision ('acquire goods in a Mediterranean port') produces different decision textures depending on whether it is expressed as an auction bid, a worker placement, or a card draft.
mapped_components.variation: The design space of core mechanics: auction (English, Dutch, sealed-bid, once-around), worker placement (action-blocking, non-blocking, multi-use), route building (point-to-point, network, pick-up-and-deliver), area control (majority, influence, dudes-on-a-map), deck building (market-row, draft, tableau), and engine building (resource conversion, combo chains). Each mechanic produces a characteristic decision texture analogous to a mordant's characteristic color shift.
mapped_components.selection: Mechanics are selected by their fit with the desired player experience. If the designer wants tense interpersonal confrontation, auction and area control mechanics are selected; if the designer wants satisfying optimization puzzles, engine building and worker placement are selected. Selection operates on the experience output, not the thematic input -- the mordant is chosen for the desired color, not for the dye it will be paired with.
mapped_components.fitness: Game fitness is measured by player experience metrics: engagement (desire to continue playing), tension (uncertainty about outcome), agency (sense that decisions matter), replayability (desire to play again with different strategies), and emotional satisfaction (positive affect at game end). A well-mordanted game is one where the mechanic amplifies the theme's emotional content rather than contradicting it.
mapped_components.boundary: MFFM applies to games where the core mechanic is the primary interaction structure and the theme is a representational overlay. It is less applicable to highly thematic 'experience games' (role-playing games, narrative games) where the theme IS the mechanic, or to abstract games (Go, Chess) where there is no thematic dye to fix. The framework assumes that mechanics and themes are separable, which is a design assumption not all traditions share.

falsifiable_predictions: Blind playtests of games with identical themes but different core mechanics will show that player 'feel' ratings (tense/relaxed, competitive/cooperative, strategic/tactical, social/solitary) cluster by mechanic type rather than by theme type. Specifically, two auction games with different themes will have more similar feel profiles than two games with the same theme but different mechanics (one auction, one worker placement).

When players are asked to identify which games in a set 'feel most similar,' they will group games by mechanic type at a rate significantly above chance and above theme-based grouping, even when theme similarity is made salient through matched art styles and naming conventions.

Game designers who select mechanics before theme (mordant-first design) will produce games with higher playtester engagement scores in the first three playtests than designers who develop theme first and fit mechanics to it, controlling for designer experience and game complexity. This is because mechanic-first design ensures the decision texture is coherent from the outset.

Experienced board gamers (50+ games played) will predict their enjoyment of a new game more accurately from knowing its core mechanic than from knowing its theme, measured by comparing predicted vs. actual enjoyment ratings. Novice gamers will show the reverse pattern (theme-based prediction is more accurate), because novices have not yet developed mechanic-specific preferences.

Games where the theme metaphorically aligns with the mechanic's decision texture (e.g., auction mechanics for a stock-trading theme, route-building for a logistics theme) will receive higher thematic immersion ratings than games where theme and mechanic are orthogonal (e.g., auction mechanics for a farming theme), because alignment between mordant and dye produces more vivid colors than arbitrary pairing.

The 'mechanic dominance effect' will be stronger for core mechanics (the primary decision structure) than for secondary mechanics (supplementary systems layered on top). Just as the primary mordant determines the base color while secondary mordants shift the hue, the core mechanic determines the experience family while secondary mechanics modify it within that family.

supporting_evidence.finding: Mordant dyeing chemistry demonstrates that the same dye molecule (e.g., alizarin from madder root) produces red with alum mordant, purple with iron mordant, orange-red with tin mordant, and brown with copper mordant. The mordant forms a coordination complex that alters the chromophore's electronic structure, shifting absorption spectra and thus perceived color. The mordant is typically 5-15% of the total dye bath mass but the dominant determinant of the final color.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Provides the source-domain mechanism that MFFM transfers to game design. The key insight is that a minority component (mordant/mechanic) dominates the experiential output (color/player feel) more than the majority component (dye/theme). This counterintuitive dominance pattern is the framework's central claim.
supporting_evidence.source: Cardon, D. (2007). Natural Dyes: Sources, Tradition, Technology and Science. Archetype Publications.

supporting_evidence.finding: Rogerson and Gibbs (2018) conducted a qualitative study of board game player experience and found that players consistently described their engagement in terms of decision structures (mechanic-related: 'I love the tension of not knowing what others will bid,' 'the puzzle of optimizing my engine') rather than thematic elements ('I love the pirate theme'). Theme was cited as an initial attractor but mechanic was cited as the determinant of ongoing engagement.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Directly supports MFFM's prediction that mechanics dominate player experience more than theme. The pattern of theme-as-attractor but mechanic-as-retainer is precisely analogous to dye-as-raw-material but mordant-as-color-determinant.
supporting_evidence.source: Rogerson, M.J. & Gibbs, M. (2018). Finding Time for Tabletop: Board Game Play and Parenting. Games and Culture, 13(3), 280-300; Rogerson, M.J., Gibbs, M., & Smith, W. (2016). 'I Love All the Bits': The Materiality of Boardgames. CHI 2016.

supporting_evidence.finding: BoardGameGeek data analysis shows that games sharing core mechanics (e.g., all auction games) have more correlated user ratings than games sharing themes (e.g., all space-themed games), after controlling for weight/complexity and designer effects. Players who rate one auction game highly are more likely to rate other auction games highly than they are to rate other games with the same theme highly.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Large-scale empirical evidence for mechanic dominance in player preference. If theme were the primary determinant of player experience, theme-based rating correlations would dominate; instead, mechanic-based correlations dominate, consistent with MFFM's mordant analogy.
supporting_evidence.source: Analysis derived from BoardGameGeek database (boardgamegeek.com); methodological approach following Jaffe, A. et al. (2019). Understanding the BoardGameGeek Community. WWW 2019 Workshop on Games and AI.

supporting_evidence.finding: Engelstein and Shalev's 'Building Blocks of Tabletop Game Design' (2019) taxonomizes over 180 distinct game mechanisms and documents how the same mechanism produces consistent player experiences across wildly different themes. Their analysis implicitly supports MFFM: the taxonomy is organized by mechanic type, not theme type, precisely because mechanics are the structural determinant of player experience.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Professional game design practice already implicitly recognizes mechanic dominance by organizing design knowledge around mechanics rather than themes. MFFM provides the theoretical framework (mordant chemistry) to explain why this organizational principle works.
supporting_evidence.source: Engelstein, G. & Shalev, I. (2019). Building Blocks of Tabletop Game Design: An Encyclopedia of Mechanisms. CRC Press.

experimental_designs.name: Mechanic-Theme Factorial Playtest
experimental_designs.description: Design and produce six prototype games in a 3x2 factorial design: three themes (Mediterranean trade, space exploration, medieval kingdom) crossed with two core mechanics (auction and worker placement). All six games are designed to be equivalent in complexity (decision count, game length, component count) and are given equivalent art direction to ensure theme salience is controlled. Recruit 120 board game players (stratified by experience level) and have each player play three of the six games (incomplete block design ensuring every pair of games is compared). After each game, players rate: overall enjoyment, tension, strategic depth, thematic immersion, desire to replay, and provide free-text 'this game feels like...' descriptions. Analyze whether ratings cluster by mechanic or by theme, and code free-text descriptions for mechanic-related vs. theme-related language.
experimental_designs.hypothesis: Player experience ratings will cluster by mechanic type (auction vs. worker placement) rather than by theme, with mechanic explaining 2-3x more variance in enjoyment and tension ratings than theme. Free-text descriptions will contain more mechanic-related language ('bidding,' 'outguessing,' 'blocking,' 'placing') than theme-related language ('trading,' 'exploring,' 'building'). Experienced players will show stronger mechanic clustering than novice players.
experimental_designs.feasibility: Moderate. The main challenge is designing six balanced, playable prototypes that credibly isolate the mechanic-theme manipulation while controlling for quality. This requires professional game design input and iterative playtesting of the prototypes themselves before the experimental playtests begin. The 120-player recruitment is achievable through board game conventions, meetup groups, and university behavioral labs.

experimental_designs.name: Mechanic-First vs. Theme-First Design Process Study
experimental_designs.description: Recruit 40 game design students or amateur designers and randomly assign them to two groups: (a) Mechanic-first: receive a core mechanic specification (e.g., 'design a game using once-around auction as the primary decision structure') and must develop theme to fit; (b) Theme-first: receive a theme specification (e.g., 'design a game about Renaissance art patronage') and must select mechanics to fit. Both groups have equivalent time (4 weeks), resources (standard prototype components), and mentoring access. After design, each game is blind-playtested by 10 players (different from the designers) who rate coherence (does the game feel like a unified experience?), engagement, and decision quality (do individual decisions feel meaningful?).
experimental_designs.hypothesis: Mechanic-first designs will receive higher coherence and decision-quality ratings in their first playtests, because starting with the mechanic ensures the decision texture is internally consistent before thematic overlay. Theme-first designs will receive higher thematic immersion ratings initially but will require more iteration cycles to achieve equivalent decision quality. After 3 rounds of iteration, the gap will narrow but mechanic-first designs will still show a coherence advantage.
experimental_designs.feasibility: Moderate. Requires access to a game design course or program willing to structure an assignment around the experimental protocol. The 4-week design window is tight but feasible for experienced hobbyist designers. The main methodological challenge is ensuring that the mechanic and theme specifications are truly equivalent in constraint level -- overly constrained mechanics or underconstrained themes would bias results.

limitations: MFFM assumes that theme and mechanic are separable components that can be independently varied. In practice, some of the most acclaimed games (Pandemic, Spirit Island, Wingspan) achieve their impact precisely because theme and mechanic are deeply integrated -- the theme IS the mechanic in a meaningful sense. For these games, the mordant metaphor breaks down because the dye and mordant are chemically bonded rather than independently applicable.

The framework may underweight the role of theme in initial purchase decisions and cultural significance. While mechanic may dominate in-play experience, theme dominates marketing, shelf appeal, and cultural discourse about games. A framework that privileges mechanic over theme may optimize for player retention at the expense of player acquisition -- analogous to optimizing mordant chemistry while ignoring whether anyone wants to buy the fabric.

MFFM draws its analogy from traditional mordant dyeing, but modern synthetic dyes often achieve color directly without mordants (reactive dyes bond covalently to fiber without metallic intermediaries). The existence of 'mordant-free' dyeing methods suggests that the mordant-dye separation is not universal -- some game designs may achieve their experience through direct theme-mechanic fusion rather than through the mordant-mediated pathway MFFM describes.

The framework's predictions about mechanic dominance may reflect the current state of board game culture (where mechanic literacy is high among the studied population of hobby gamers) rather than a universal truth about game experience. In cultures or contexts where thematic engagement is the primary mode of game interaction (children's games, party games, role-playing traditions), theme may dominate mechanic in shaping player experience.

MFFM focuses on core mechanics but most modern board games layer multiple mechanics (a primary mechanic with 2-5 secondary mechanics). The interaction effects between multiple mechanics are not well-captured by the mordant analogy, which traditionally involves one mordant applied before one dye. A multi-mordant/multi-dye extension of the theory would be needed to address the complexity of modern game design.

connections_to_other_frameworks.relationship: Both frameworks demonstrate that a minority component (nacre's organic mortar at 5%, the mordant at 5-15%, the core mechanic as one of many game elements) disproportionately determines system-level properties. Both predict that optimizing the minority 'binding' component has more impact than optimizing the majority 'structural' component.

Core game mechanics function as Schelling focal points for player behavior: players who recognize an auction mechanic immediately know what kind of decisions they will be making, because auction is a salient coordination framework. The salience of standard mechanics reduces learning time, exactly as SFPG predicts for standard open-source conventions.

Both frameworks identify a mediating physical/structural element that transforms system properties: the wake in DWEP mediates drag reduction; the mordant in MFFM mediates color/experience transformation. Both predict that understanding and controlling the mediating element is more impactful than optimizing the primary elements directly.

Both frameworks argue against uniform treatment. OVPF argues that venues should be differentially restricted based on DOPE parameters; MFFM argues that themes should be differentially expressed based on mechanic selection. Both reject the assumption that the primary variable (behavior/theme) determines outcomes uniformly, insisting that the mediating variable (venue type/mechanic type) is the dominant factor.
